5.4.3	Minimum Flow

nodes.text:
* It was assumed that there are {{ No/Minimal }} significant industrial dischargers whose batch discharges or unique operational schedules could disproportionately affect or create atypical peak flows outside the normal diurnal pattern. This is based on {{ Rationale_Industrial, for instance, a review of connected users and discharge permits }}. <<check>>
* The applied peaking factor is representative of the combined diurnal wastewater generation patterns for the mix of users in {{ Name_of_Town/Utility }}. <<check>>

### 5.4.3 Minimum Flow

Calculating daily minimum flows is necessary to ensure that self-cleansing velocities are maintained within the gravity portions of the sewer collection system, preventing the deposition of solids and subsequent blockages, odor issues, and corrosion. It is also important for the proper operation of pumps (to avoid excessive short-cycling which can damage equipment) and some treatment processes that may be sensitive to very low flow conditions or require a minimum hydraulic throughput.

Based on the inspection of flow data from 2022 to 2024, the minimum daily influent volume for the period under consideration was on August 23ʳᵈ, 2024. This value was divided by the 2024 ERUs to produce a minimum daily flow per ERU to arrive at a value of {{ Minimum_Daily_Flow_per_ERU }} gpd/ERU, or approximately 24.2 gpm per capita (assuming {{ Avg_Persons_per_ERU }} persons/ERU).

To estimate a "peak hour minimum flow," or more accurately, the lowest instantaneous flow expected during the diurnal cycle on a minimum flow day, the minimum average daily flow rate is considered. Even during the day with the lowest total flow, there will still be diurnal variations with very low flows typically occurring in the late-night/early-morning hours. <<check>> This can be estimated using a minimum diurnal factor **(for instance, {{ Minimum_Diurnal_Factor_Value, for instance, 0.25 to 0.5 }} times the average flow on that minimum day)**. This minimum instantaneous flow is the critical condition for assessing if velocities in sewers are sufficient to prevent solids deposition, especially in flatter gradient pipes.

Ensuring adequate cleansing velocity (typically around 2 feet per second when flowing at least half full for sanitary sewers) at minimum flow conditions is important because it helps maintain the operational efficiency and longevity of the collection system. It prevents the buildup of solids, which can lead to reduced pipe capacity, accelerated corrosion from septic conditions, nuisance odor generation, and an increased need for costly and disruptive sewer cleaning operations.

### 5.4.4 Dry Weather Flow

Average Dry Weather Flow (ADWF) is defined as the total wastewater flow entering the treatment facility during an extended dry period, typically lasting 7 to 14 consecutive days, when direct rainfall-induced inflow is negligible. This flow consists of the true sanitary flow (Qs) generated by residential, commercial, and institutional users, combined with groundwater infiltration (QGWI) that may seep into the collection system through pipe defects, joints, or manholes, especially when groundwater levels are seasonally high. It explicitly excludes significant, direct commercial or industrial process discharges that are not part of the baseline sanitary load, if any.
nodes.text_raw:
* It was assumed that there are {{ No/Minimal }} significant industrial dischargers whose batch discharges or unique operational schedules could disproportionately affect or create atypical peak flows outside the normal diurnal pattern. This is based on {{ Rationale_Industrial, for instance, a review of connected users and discharge permits }}. <<check>>
* The applied peaking factor is representative of the combined diurnal wastewater generation patterns for the mix of users in {{ Name_of_Town/Utility }}. <<check>>

### 5.4.3 Minimum Flow

Calculating daily minimum flows is necessary to ensure that self-cleansing velocities are maintained within the gravity portions of the sewer collection system, preventing the deposition of solids and subsequent blockages, odor issues, and corrosion. It is also important for the proper operation of pumps (to avoid excessive short-cycling which can damage equipment) and some treatment processes that may be sensitive to very low flow conditions or require a minimum hydraulic throughput.

Based on the inspection of flow data from 2022 to 2024, the minimum daily influent volume for the period under consideration was on August 23ʳᵈ, 2024. This value was divided by the 2024 ERUs to produce a minimum daily flow per ERU to arrive at a value of {{ Minimum_Daily_Flow_per_ERU }} gpd/ERU, or approximately 24.2 gpm per capita (assuming {{ Avg_Persons_per_ERU }} persons/ERU).

To estimate a "peak hour minimum flow," or more accurately, the lowest instantaneous flow expected during the diurnal cycle on a minimum flow day, the minimum average daily flow rate is considered. Even during the day with the lowest total flow, there will still be diurnal variations with very low flows typically occurring in the late-night/early-morning hours. <<check>> This can be estimated using a minimum diurnal factor **(for instance, {{ Minimum_Diurnal_Factor_Value, for instance, 0.25 to 0.5 }} times the average flow on that minimum day)**. This minimum instantaneous flow is the critical condition for assessing if velocities in sewers are sufficient to prevent solids deposition, especially in flatter gradient pipes.

Ensuring adequate cleansing velocity (typically around 2 feet per second when flowing at least half full for sanitary sewers) at minimum flow conditions is important because it helps maintain the operational efficiency and longevity of the collection system. It prevents the buildup of solids, which can lead to reduced pipe capacity, accelerated corrosion from septic conditions, nuisance odor generation, and an increased need for costly and disruptive sewer cleaning operations.

### 5.4.4 Dry Weather Flow

Average Dry Weather Flow (ADWF) is defined as the total wastewater flow entering the treatment facility during an extended dry period, typically lasting 7 to 14 consecutive days, when direct rainfall-induced inflow is negligible. This flow consists of the true sanitary flow (Qs) generated by residential, commercial, and institutional users, combined with groundwater infiltration (QGWI) that may seep into the collection system through pipe defects, joints, or manholes, especially when groundwater levels are seasonally high. It explicitly excludes significant, direct commercial or industrial process discharges that are not part of the baseline sanitary load, if any.

To estimate a "peak hour minimum flow," or more accurately, the lowest instantaneous flow expected during the diurnal cycle on a minimum flow day, the minimum average daily flow rate is considered. Even during the day with the lowest total flow, there will still be diurnal variations with very low flows typically occurring in the late-night/early-morning hours. <<check>> This can be estimated using a minimum diurnal factor **(for instance, {{ Minimum_Diurnal_Factor_Value, for instance, 0.25 to 0.5 }} times the average flow on that minimum day)**. This minimum instantaneous flow is the critical condition for assessing if velocities in sewers are sufficient to prevent solids deposition, especially in flatter gradient pipes. Ensuring adequate cleansing velocity (typically around 2 feet per second when flowing at least half full for sanitary sewers) at minimum flow conditions is important because it helps maintain the operational efficiency and longevity of the collection system. It prevents the buildup of solids, which can lead to reduced pipe capacity, accelerated corrosion from septic conditions, nuisance odor generation, and an increased need for costly and disruptive sewer cleaning operations. ### 5.4.4 Dry Weather Flow Average Dry Weather Flow (ADWF) is defined as the total wastewater flow entering the treatment facility during an extended dry period, typically lasting 7 to 14 consecutive days, when direct rainfall-induced inflow is negligible. This flow consists of the true sanitary flow (Qs) generated by residential, commercial, and institutional users, combined with groundwater infiltration (QGWI) that may seep into the collection system through pipe defects, joints, or manholes, especially when groundwater levels are seasonally high. It explicitly excludes significant, direct commercial or industrial process discharges that are not part of the baseline sanitary load, if any.

nodes.text:
Table 5.3-3: Summary of Rainfall Data for Peak Flow Events
Date Rainfall Depth (inches) Notes
June 13, 2022 0.66 Occurred during a rainfall event from 6/10 to 6/14, two days after a 1.06 inch precipitation event on 6/7
December 2, 2023 0.54 Occurred during a rainfall event from 12/1 to 12/12
January 2, 2024 0.05 Occurred after a 0.5 in rainfall event on 12/31/23
## 5.3.3(a) Peak Hour Flow Calculation The peak hour flow was calculated by first identifying the highest recorded daily wastewater volume from the 2022 to 2024 sample period. The highest daily volume observed was 1.18 MG on June 13ᵗʰ, 2022. When attributed to the number of active ERUs in 2022, it translates **25,650 gpd/ERU**. This daily volume per ERU is then converted to an average daily flow rate in gallons per minute (GPM) for a flow rate of . To estimate the peak hour flow from this highest average daily flow rate, a diurnal peaking factor is applied. This factor accounts for the typical variations in water use -----------------------0s }}, a peaking factor of {{ Peaking_Factor_Value, for instance, using the Harmon formula, Babbitt formula, or a typical range like 2.5 to 4.0 for smaller systems }} was used. Therefore, Peak Hour Flow (GPM) = (Highest Daily Flow Rate in GPM) ×[; ‘me_of_Town/Utility }}, this results in a calculated Peak Hour Flow of {{ ‘/8 }} GPM. ## 5.3.3(b) Assumptions and Simplifications The following assumptions were made in the derivation of the peak hour flow calculations: * It was assumed that contributions from {{ Commercial_Institutional_Sources_Assumption, for instance, commercial and institutional sources within the service area exhibit similar diurnal peaking patterns to residential flows, or their contribution to the overall peak flow is relatively small and thus adequately accounted for within the selected overall peaking factor }}. This is because {{ Rationale_Commercial, for instance, the service area is predominantly residential with limited large commercial/institutional users that would have significantly different peak discharge times }}. <>

Peak Wet Weather Flow (PWWF) 0.39 57.8 86.1 114.7 149.4
Peak Hour Design Flow (PHDF) 0.44 108.0 224.8 383.0 626.0
Peak Instantaneous Design Flow (PIDF) 0.48 118.8 247.3 421.3 688.6
Minimum Flow 0.02 2.5 3.7 5.0 3.2
## 5.6 Peaking Factors Based on the provided flow data, flow factors were calculated by comparing each flow parameter to the Average Daily Flow (ADF) to determine variations in system demand. The Peaking Factors were derived by taking the ratio of peak flows, such as Peak Hour Design Flow (PHDF) and Peak Wet Weather Flow (PWWF), to ADF, highlighting the expected surges in wastewater generation. The Maximum Month Flow (MMF) Factor represents the seasonal variations, while the Maximum Dry Weather Flow (MDWF) Factor accounts for daily dry weather peak conditions. Additionally, the Minimum Flow Factor indicates the lowest expected flow relative to ADF. These factors were averaged over the period from 2025 to 2045 to provide a representative assessment of system performance. The summarized flow ratios are presented in the table below:
TABLE EXISTING AND PROJECTED WASTEWATER CHARACTERISTICS.21- PEAKING FACTORS
Flow Factor Average Ratio
(2025–2045)
Peak Wet Weather Flow Factor (PWWF/ADF) 6.91
Maximum Month Flow Factor (MMF/ADF) 2.44
Maximum Dry Weather Flow Factor (MDWF/ADF) 1.55
Peak Hourly Peaking Factor (PHDF/ADF) 7.73
Peak Instantaneous Design Flow (PIDF)* 8.50
Minimum Flow Factor (Min Flow/ADF) 0.30
*It was assumed that the instantaneous peak design flow is 10% higher than the peak hourly design flow. ## 5.7 Wastewater Influent Characterization Spangle's discharge monitoring reports provide data on influent pollutant concentrations, including BOD₅, TSS, and NH₃ loadings. However, the location where samples are collected may impact the accuracy of the data and the representation of influent characteristics. This is because the facility’s scum removal device on the secondary clarifier recycles cleaner water back to the

nodes.text:
TABLE EXISTING AND PROJECTED WASTEWATER CHARACTERISTICS.15: 72-HOUR RAINFALL DATA – DECEMBER 2022
Date Rainfall Depth (in)
December 25, 2022 0.21
December 26, 2022 1.05
December 27, 2022 0.98
### Peak Hour Flow Calculation Highest Daily Volume: 55652 gallons or 391.9 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 142 for the year 2022, refer to Table5.2). The highest daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.27 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.27 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.44 \, \text{gpm} $$ **Assumptions and Simplifications** Since the service area does not have significant contributions from commercial or institutional users, it is assumed that the peak hourly flow occurs simultaneously across all sources. This eliminates the need to calculate peak hourly flow separately for different entities, avoiding potential overestimation of the total peak flow. By accounting for these factors, the peak hourly flow estimation provides a conservative design for the sewer system's critical infrastructure. #### 5.3.4 Minimum Flow Average daily minimum flows are used to determine if velocities are adequate to prevent the buildup of solids in the sewer. The recorded minimum daily volume to the treatment plant is 3,146 gpd, which occurred on September 7, 2020. Minimum Daily Volume: 3146 gallons or 22.96 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 137 for the year 2020) The minimum daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.016 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.016 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.025 \, \text{gpm} $$ The logic behind using the maximum multiplier for the minimum flow is that it helps estimate the highest flow during the minimum daily flow period, which is essential for ensuring the self-cleansing velocity of the sewer system over a 24-hour period. The self-cleansing velocity is the minimum velocity required to prevent solids from settling in the sewer pipes. By applying the multiplier, we account for potential flow within the day, ensuring that even during periods of low flow, the system can effectively transport solids and prevent blockages. This approach helps

TABLE EXISTING AND PROJECTED WASTEWATER CHARACTERISTICS.15: 72-HOUR RAINFALL DATA – DECEMBER 2022
Date Rainfall Depth (in)
December 25, 2022 0.21
December 26, 2022 1.05
December 27, 2022 0.98
### Peak Hour Flow Calculation Highest Daily Volume: 55652 gallons or 391.9 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 142 for the year 2022, refer to Table5.2). The highest daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.27 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.27 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.44 \, \text{gpm} $$ **Assumptions and Simplifications** Since the service area does not have significant contributions from commercial or institutional users, it is assumed that the peak hourly flow occurs simultaneously across all sources. This eliminates the need to calculate peak hourly flow separately for different entities, avoiding potential overestimation of the total peak flow. By accounting for these factors, the peak hourly flow estimation provides a conservative design for the sewer system's critical infrastructure. #### 5.3.4 Minimum Flow Average daily minimum flows are used to determine if velocities are adequate to prevent the buildup of solids in the sewer. The recorded minimum daily volume to the treatment plant is 3,146 gpd, which occurred on September 7, 2020. Minimum Daily Volume: 3146 gallons or 22.96 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 137 for the year 2020) The minimum daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.016 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.016 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.025 \, \text{gpm} $$ The logic behind using the maximum multiplier for the minimum flow is that it helps estimate the highest flow during the minimum daily flow period, which is essential for ensuring the self-cleansing velocity of the sewer system over a 24-hour period. The self-cleansing velocity is the minimum velocity required to prevent solids from settling in the sewer pipes. By applying the multiplier, we account for potential flow within the day, ensuring that even during periods of low flow, the system can effectively transport solids and prevent blockages. This approach helps

TABLE EXISTING AND PROJECTED WASTEWATER CHARACTERISTICS.15: 72-HOUR RAINFALL DATA – DECEMBER 2022
Date Rainfall Depth (in)
December 25, 2022 0.21
December 26, 2022 1.05
December 27, 2022 0.98
### Peak Hour Flow Calculation Highest Daily Volume: 55652 gallons or 391.9 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 142 for the year 2022, refer to Table5.2). The highest daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.27 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.27 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.44 \, \text{gpm} $$ **Assumptions and Simplifications** Since the service area does not have significant contributions from commercial or institutional users, it is assumed that the peak hourly flow occurs simultaneously across all sources. This eliminates the need to calculate peak hourly flow separately for different entities, avoiding potential overestimation of the total peak flow. By accounting for these factors, the peak hourly flow estimation provides a conservative design for the sewer system's critical infrastructure. #### 5.3.4 Minimum Flow Average daily minimum flows are used to determine if velocities are adequate to prevent the buildup of solids in the sewer. The recorded minimum daily volume to the treatment plant is 3,146 gpd, which occurred on September 7, 2020. Minimum Daily Volume: 3146 gallons or 22.96 gpd /ERU (Total ERU= 137 for the year 2020) The minimum daily flow translates to an average daily flow rate of **0.016 gallons per minute (gpm).** To estimate the peak hourly flow, a diurnal multiplier of **1.6** was applied to the highest daily average flow rate: $$ \text{Peak Hour Flow} = 0.016 \, \text{gpm} \times 1.6 = 0.025 \, \text{gpm} $$ The logic behind using the maximum multiplier for the minimum flow is that it helps estimate the highest flow during the minimum daily flow period, which is essential for ensuring the self-cleansing velocity of the sewer system over a 24-hour period. The self-cleansing velocity is the minimum velocity required to prevent solids from settling in the sewer pipes. By applying the multiplier, we account for potential flow within the day, ensuring that even during periods of low flow, the system can effectively transport solids and prevent blockages. This approach helps

nodes.text:
# 5.3 Wastewater Flow Summary *WAC 173-240-050(3)(g)* Discharge Monitoring Reports (DMRs) from 2022 through 2024 were analyzed to determine historical influent flows to WWTP for the design period. This data, which represents actual measured flows to and from the facility (taken daily), provides the basis for characterizing system hydraulics in this report. The flow data along with OFM population data, was used to generate **Table 5.3-1**, which summarizes total system flows, average daily flows, estimated total Equivalent Residential Units (ERUs), and the average daily flow per ERU. The table is followed by a
Year Total Flow Entire System
(MG/Year)		 Average Daily Flow (ADF)
(gpd)		 Total Equivalent Residential Units (ERU) Average Daily Flow Per ERU
(gpd/ERU)
2022 49.1 134,520 460 292
2023 42.87 117,452 460 255
2024 36.8 100,486 461 218
Guidance for ERU estimation and flow analysis was taken from {{ Cite_Guidance_Document, for instance, Washington State Department of Ecology's "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book) }}. The Orange Book provides standardized methodologies and typical values used in Washington State, ensuring consistency in engineering assessments. <> The average annual flow to the WWTP over this period was calculated to be {{ Calculated_Average_Annual_Flow }} gpd. This value is utilized for long-term operational planning, estimating annual O&M costs, and as a fundamental baseline for developing flow projections and assessing overall plant loading against its design capacity.

nodes.text:
The goals of the manual are: 1. To ensure that the design of sewage collection and treatment systems is consistent with public health, water quality, and biosolids management objectives of Washington State. 2. To establish a basis for the design and review of plans and specifications for sewage treatment works and sewerage systems. 3. To establish the minimum requirements and limiting factors utilized by the Washington Department of Ecology and the Washington State Department of Health for review of sewage treatment works and sewerage system plans and specifications. 4. To assist the owner or the authorized engineer in the preparation of plans, specifications, reports, and other data. 5. To guide departments in their determination of whether an approval, permit, and/or a certificate for a sewage treatment works or sewerage system should be issued. The CSWD provides guidance, prepared by Ecology, for the benefit of agency staff and the designers, owners, and operators of sewage treatment works and sewerage systems. The intent of the CSWD manual is to address requirements that will lead to approvable plans and specifications for sewage treatment works and sewerage systems. Although this manual is not regulation, state regulation (WAC 173-240-040) requires reasonable consistency with the CSWD requirements. Ecology intends to revise sections of this manual as updated information becomes available. Ecology will make the revisions available electronically. A list of the revisions with dates is provided below. ## List of recent revisions Ecology has made the following revisions to the 1998 publication: ### 2006 Updates and Revisions * Purpose of Manual (Introduction) * State Agencies (G1-1.4.1D) * Environmental Review; SEPA, NEPA, SERP (G1-2.6) * Operation and Maintenance Manual (G1-4.4) * Design loading, treatment plants (G2-1.2) * Ponds and Aerated Lagoons (G3-3.5) * Sewer System Rehabilitation/Replacement Techniques (C1-8) * Special Requirements (C1-9) * MBR (New section in Treatment Chapter) * Residual Solids Management (Chapter S) * Treatment Technologies (E1-4) * Distribution and Storage (E1-5) Ecology appreciates contributions and reviews from the following firms and local government agencies:
King CountyLakehaven Utility District
Enviroquip.Gray & Osborne
Kennedy JenksBrown & Caldwell
ZenonHDR
### 2007 Updates and Revisions \n---\n # Criteria for Sewage Works Design - General Engineering Requirements (Table G1‑2 Explanation of Engineering Report Requirements) - General Engineering Requirements (Chapter G1 – Section G1‑5.4.2 Facility Rerating Procedures) - Sewers (Chapter C1‑7 – Assessment of Structural Conditions and Infiltrations/Inflow) - Biological Treatment (Chapter T3 – Section T3‑3.1.2 Batch Treatment [Sequencing Batch Reactor]) - Disinfection (Chapter T5 – Section T5‑2 Ultraviolet Light [Sub‑sections Tr‑2.2, T5‑2.3, T5‑2.4, T5‑2.5, and T5‑2.6]) 2008 Updates and Revisions - General Considerations (Chapter G2 – Sections 2 and 5) - Sewers (Chapter C1 – Sections 5 and 10) - Sewage Pump Stations (Chapter C2 – Section 3) - Preliminary Treatment/Septage and Other Liquid Hauled Wastes (Chapter T1 – Section 1) - Water Reclamation and Reuse (Chapter E1 – Added Section 5.2.7) - Residual Solids Management (Chapter S – Sections 1 and 2) \n---\n # Acknowledgements (1998) This manual was developed for the Washington State Department of Ecology with the generous assistance of many government agencies, sewer and water districts, consulting engineering firms, and wastewater treatment professionals. ## Editorial/Coordination Board members * Louthain, Jerry (Project Manager) * Berschauer, Doug * Cole, Mark * Foulk, Jamie * Lowe, Susan * Meriwether, Cam * Nitz, Bill * Olson, Karen * Perry, Don * Skillings, Tom * Vivolo, Tony ## Chapter G3, Special Considerations for Small Community Systems * Meriwether, Cam (Coordinator) * Milton, Jim (Leader) * Alexander, Ken * Arts, Chris * Bergstrom, Eric * Berschauer, Doug * Johnstone, Charles * Nichols, Don * Nunnallee, David * Riegert, Larry * Riley, Dick * Sherwood, Kim * Skillings, Tom * Tom, Andy * Vivolo, Tony ## Chapter C1, Sewers ## Chapter C2, Sewage Pump Stations * Vivolo, Tony (Coordinator) * Thetford, Tom (Leader) * Bickel, Scott * Brown, Lisa * Browne, Roger * Cardwell, Bruce * Carlsen, Jim * Chambers, Craig * Davison, Eric * Eliasson, John * Gagnon, Richard * Hammond,

nodes.text:
The process design of treatment units should be based on either the average design flow or the peak design flow, whichever is controlling. The following items should be determined from the observed rate of flow during the significant period of discharge. Items to be considered in determining design flows are as follows: * Peak flow rates continuing over a length of time sufficient to adversely affect the detention time of treatment units or the flow characteristics in conduits. * Applicable data from similar municipalities. * Wet weather flows. * Recirculation and inplant recycle flows. The design organic loading should be computed in the same manner used in determining design flow. ## G2-1.2.3 Existing Systems Treatment plants designed to serve existing sewerage systems should be designed on the basis of characteristics of sewage obtained from the operating records of the treatment works. The design engineer or owner shall provide a plan acceptable to Ecology for eliminating or handling excessive inflow/infiltration (I/I) so that there will be no discharge of inadequately treated wastewaters or impairment of the treatment process. ## G2-1.2.4 New Systems Sewage treatment plants to serve new sewerage systems should be designed on the basis of information in Table G2‑ 2. Numbers of persons per dwelling should be based on planning projections derived from an official source. Any deviations should be based on sound engineering judgment substantiated in the engineering report. Table G2- 2. Design Basis for New Sewage Works ## Table G2- 2. Design Basis for New Sewage Works
